The **Indonesia–Malaysia confrontation** or **Borneo confrontation** (known as ***Konfrontasi*** in Indonesia, Malaysia and Singapore) was an armed conflict from 1963 to 1966 that stemmed from [Indonesia](/wiki/Indonesia "Indonesia")'s opposition to the creation of the state of [Malaysia](/wiki/Malaysia "Malaysia"). Vital precursors to the conflict included Indonesia's [policy of confrontation](/wiki/West_New_Guinea_dispute "West New Guinea dispute") against [Dutch New Guinea](/wiki/Dutch_New_Guinea "Dutch New Guinea") from March to August 1962 and the Indonesia-backed [Brunei revolt](/wiki/Brunei_revolt "Brunei revolt") in December 1962. Malaysia had direct military support from the United Kingdom, Australia, and New Zealand. Indonesia had indirect support from the USSR and China, thus making it an episode of the [Cold War in Asia](/wiki/Cold_War_in_Asia "Cold War in Asia").

The conflict was an [undeclared war](/wiki/Undeclared_war "Undeclared war") with most of the action occurring in the border area between Indonesia and East Malaysia on the island of [Borneo](/wiki/Borneo "Borneo") (known as [Kalimantan](/wiki/Kalimantan "Kalimantan") in Indonesia). However Indonesia also conducted lower intensity covert actions on the [Malay Peninsula](/wiki/Malay_Peninsula "Malay Peninsula") and in Singapore. The conflict was characterised by restrained and isolated ground combat, set within tactics of low-level [brinkmanship](/wiki/Brinkmanship "Brinkmanship"). Combat was usually conducted by [company](/wiki/Company_(military_unit) "Company (military unit)")- or [platoon](/wiki/Platoon "Platoon")-sized operations on either side of the border. Indonesia's campaign of infiltrations into Borneo sought to exploit how ethnically and religiously diverse Sabah and Sarawak were compared to that of Malaya and Singapore, with the intent of unravelling the proposed state of Malaysia.

The jungle terrain of Borneo and the lack of roads straddling the [Indonesia–Malaysia border](/wiki/Indonesia%E2%80%93Malaysia_border "Indonesia–Malaysia border") forced both Indonesian and Commonwealth forces to conduct long foot patrols. Both sides relied on light infantry operations and air transport, although Commonwealth forces enjoyed the advantage of better helicopter deployment and resupply to forward operating bases. Rivers were also used as a method of transport and infiltration. Although combat operations were primarily conducted by ground forces, airborne forces played a vital support role and naval forces ensured the security of the sea flanks. The [British](/wiki/British_Armed_Forces "British Armed Forces") provided most of the defensive effort, although Malaysian forces steadily increased their contributions, and there were periodic contributions from [Australian](/wiki/Australian_Defence_Force "Australian Defence Force") and [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand_Defence_Force "New Zealand Defence Force") forces within the combined [Far East Strategic Reserve](/wiki/Far_East_Strategic_Reserve "Far East Strategic Reserve") stationed then in Peninsular Malaysia and Singapore.[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDennisGrey199625-16)

Initially, Indonesian attacks on East Malaysia relied heavily on local volunteers trained by the [Indonesian Army](/wiki/Indonesian_Army "Indonesian Army"). Over time, the [infiltration forces](/wiki/Direct_action_(military) "Direct action (military)") became more organised with the inclusion of a more substantial component of Indonesian forces. To deter and disrupt Indonesia's growing campaign of infiltrations, the British responded in 1964 by launching their own covert operations into Indonesian Kalimantan under the code name [Operation Claret](/wiki/Operation_Claret "Operation Claret"). Claret was successful in gaining the initiative for the British Commonwealth forces, keeping the Indonesians on the defensive. Coinciding with Sukarno announcing a 'year of dangerous living' and the [1964 race riots in Singapore](/wiki/1964_race_riots_in_Singapore "1964 race riots in Singapore"), Indonesia launched an expanded campaign of operations into Peninsular Malaysia on 17 August 1964, albeit without military success.[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEdwards1992306-17) A build-up of Indonesian forces on the Kalimantan border in December 1964 saw the UK commit significant forces from the UK-based [Army Strategic Command](/wiki/Army_Strategic_Command_(United_Kingdom) "Army Strategic Command (United Kingdom)"). Australia and New Zealand deployed [roulement](/wiki/Roulement "Roulement") combat forces from Peninsular Malaysia to Borneo in 1965–66. The intensity of the conflict began to subside following the coup d'état of [October 1965](/wiki/30_September_Movement "30 September Movement") and Sukarno's loss of power to General [Suharto](/wiki/Suharto "Suharto"). A round of serious peace negotiations between the two sides began in May 1966, and a final peace agreement was signed on 11 August 1966 with Indonesia formally recognising Malaysia.[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDennisGrey1996318-18)

### Political situation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indonesia%E2%80%93Malaysia_confrontation&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Political situation")]

Before *Konfrontasi*, [Sukarno](/wiki/Sukarno "Sukarno") sought to develop an independent Indonesian foreign policy, focused on the annexation of [Dutch New Guinea](/wiki/Dutch_New_Guinea "Dutch New Guinea") as a conclusion of the [Indonesian National Revolution](/wiki/Indonesian_National_Revolution "Indonesian National Revolution"), and establishing Indonesia's credentials as a notable international power supporting its own agenda distinct from those of the [First and Second World](/wiki/Three-world_model "Three-world model"). Indonesia was an important country in developing the [Non-Aligned Movement](/wiki/Non-Aligned_Movement "Non-Aligned Movement"), hosting the [Bandung Conference](/wiki/Bandung_Conference "Bandung Conference") in 1955. Indonesia had relentlessly pursued its claim to Dutch New Guinea from 1950 to 1962, despite facing multiple setbacks in the [UN General Assembly](/wiki/UN_General_Assembly "UN General Assembly") in getting its claim recognised by the international community.

Following the Indonesian crisis in 1958, which had included the [Permesta](/wiki/Permesta "Permesta") rebellion in eastern Indonesia and the declaration of the [Revolutionary Government of the Republic of Indonesia](/wiki/Revolutionary_Government_of_the_Republic_of_Indonesia "Revolutionary Government of the Republic of Indonesia"), Indonesia had emerged as a notable and rising military power in Southeast Asia.[[17]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECain199767-19) The Central Intelligence Agency (CIA), primarily through its Taiwan-based subsidiary [Civil Air Transport](/wiki/Civil_Air_Transport "Civil Air Transport") (CAT), had been covertly providing support to rebels on remote islands, seeking to weaken, if not outright oust President Sukarno's regime. Beginning in 1957, the CIA was increasing contact with military leaders in [Sumatra](/wiki/Sumatra "Sumatra") and [Sulawesi](/wiki/Sulawesi "Sulawesi") critical of the regime. By late 1957, the transport of weapons and ammunition to Sumatra by merchant ships and night submarines had become increasingly common, but the Americans believed that for their clandestine assistance to be truly effective, such operations required the use of British facilities in Singapore to refuel and support CAT missions launched in Bangkok, Taiwan, or the Philippines.[[18]](#cite_note-EnglishHistoricalReview-Nov99-20)

With the influx of Soviet arms aid, Indonesia was able to advance its claim to Dutch New Guinea more forcefully. The diplomatic dispute reached its climax in 1962 when Indonesia launched a substantial campaign of airborne and seaborne infiltrations into Dutch New Guinea. While the infiltration forces were soundly defeated by Dutch and Papuan forces, Indonesia was able to lend credence to the threat of an Indonesian invasion of Dutch New Guinea. The Dutch, facing mounting diplomatic pressure from the Indonesians, and also the Americans, who were anxious to keep Indonesia from becoming Communist aligned, yielded and agreed to a diplomatic compromise, allowing the Indonesians to gain control of the territory in exchange for pledging to hold a self-determination plebiscite (the [Act of Free Choice](/wiki/Act_of_Free_Choice "Act of Free Choice")) in the territory by 1969. Thus by the close of 1962, Indonesia had achieved a considerable diplomatic victory, which possibly emboldened its self-perception as a notable regional power. It was in the context of this recent diplomatic victory that Indonesia cast its attention to the British proposal for a unified Malaysian state.

Before the British government announced the [East of Suez](/wiki/East_of_Suez "East of Suez") policy in 1968, it had already begun re-evaluating their commitment to maintaining a presence in the [Far East](/wiki/Far_East "Far East") as early as the late-1950s. As a part of its withdrawal from its Southeast Asian colonies, the UK moved to combine its colonies in North Borneo with the [Federation of Malaya](/wiki/Federation_of_Malaya "Federation of Malaya") (which had become independent from Britain in 1957), and Singapore (which had become self-governing in 1959). In May 1961, the UK and Malayan governments proposed a larger federation called Malaysia, encompassing the states of Malaya, [North Borneo](/wiki/Crown_Colony_of_North_Borneo "Crown Colony of North Borneo"), [Sarawak](/wiki/Crown_Colony_of_Sarawak "Crown Colony of Sarawak"), [Brunei](/wiki/Brunei "Brunei"), and [Singapore](/wiki/Singapore "Singapore"). Initially, [Indonesia](/wiki/Indonesia "Indonesia") was mildly supportive of the proposed federation, although the [Communist Party of Indonesia](/wiki/Communist_Party_of_Indonesia "Communist Party of Indonesia") (PKI) was firmly opposed to it.[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEConboy200393–95-3)

In Brunei, it was unclear whether Sultan [Omar Ali Saifuddien III](/wiki/Omar_Ali_Saifuddien_III "Omar Ali Saifuddien III") would support Brunei joining the proposed Malaysian state because of the implied reduction of his political office, and Brunei's oil revenues ensured Brunei's financial viability were it to choose independence. Furthermore, a Brunei politician, Dr. AM Azahari bin Sheikh Mahmud, while supporting a unified North Borneo, also opposed a wider Malaysian federation. In 1961, he had sounded out Indonesia about possible aid in training Borneo recruits; General [Abdul Nasution](/wiki/Abdul_Haris_Nasution "Abdul Haris Nasution") hinted at moral support, and [Soebandrio](/wiki/Soebandrio "Soebandrio"), the Indonesian foreign minister and head of intelligence, hinted at supplying more substantial aid. Azahari was a leftist who had fought in Indonesia in their war for independence.[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEConboy200393–95-3) Following these meetings, Indonesia began training a small volunteer force called the North Kalimantan National Army (TNKU) in Kalimantan.

On 8 December 1962, the TNKU staged an insurrection—the [Brunei revolt](/wiki/Brunei_revolt "Brunei revolt"). The insurrection was an abject failure, as the poorly trained and equipped forces were unable to capture key objectives such as the [Sultan of Brunei](/wiki/Sultan_of_Brunei "Sultan of Brunei"), the Brunei oil fields, or European hostages. Within hours of the insurrection being launched, British forces based in Singapore were mobilised for a prompt response. The failure of the insurrection was evident within 30 hours when [Gurkha](/wiki/Gurkha "Gurkha") troops airlifted from Singapore secured Brunei town and ensured the Sultan's safety.

The degree of Indonesian support for the TNKU remains a subject of debate. While Indonesia at the time denied direct involvement, it did sympathise with the TNKU's objectives to destabilise the proposed Malaysian state. Following the TNKU's military setback in Brunei, on 20 January 1963 Indonesian Foreign Minister [Subandrio](/wiki/Subandrio "Subandrio") announced that Indonesia would pursue a policy of *Konfrontasi* with Malaysia, reversing Indonesia's previous policy of compliance with the British proposal. This was followed by the first recorded infiltration of Indonesian forces on 12 April 1963 when a police station in Tebedu, Sarawak, was attacked.[[19]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEaster200446-21)

### People and terrain

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indonesia%E2%80%93Malaysia_confrontation&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: People and terrain")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/96/Control_of_the_island_of_Borneo.png/250px-Control_of_the_island_of_Borneo.png)](/wiki/File:Control_of_the_island_of_Borneo.png)

Borneo after cessation of hostilities; divided between Brunei, Indonesia and Malaysia. The control of the island was the main issue behind the war.

In 1961, the island of Borneo was divided between four separate entities. [Kalimantan](/wiki/Kalimantan "Kalimantan"), comprising four Indonesian [provinces](/wiki/Provinces_of_Indonesia "Provinces of Indonesia"), was located in the south of the island. In the north, were the Sultanate of Brunei (a British protectorate) and two colonies of the United Kingdom—British North Borneo (later renamed Sabah) and Sarawak. The three British territories totalled some 1.5 million people, about half of them [Dayaks](/wiki/Dayaks "Dayaks"). Sarawak had a population of about 900,000, Sabah's was 600,000 and Brunei's was around 80,000. Among Sarawak's non-Dayak population, 31% were Chinese, and 19% were Malay. Among non-Dayaks in Sabah, 21% were Chinese, and 7% were Malay; Brunei's non-Dayak population was 28% Chinese and 54% Malay. There was a large Indonesian population in Tawau in southern Sabah and a large and economically active Chinese one in Sarawak. Despite their population size, Dayaks were spread through the country in village longhouses and were not politically organised.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Sarawak was divided into five administrative divisions. Sabah, whose capital city was Jesselton ([Kota Kinabalu](/wiki/Kota_Kinabalu "Kota Kinabalu")) on the north coast, was divided into several residencies; those of the interior and Tawau were on the border. Apart from either end, the border with Indonesia generally followed a ridgeline throughout its length, rising to almost 2,500 metres in the Fifth Division. In the First Division, there were some roads, including a continuous road from Kuching to Brunei and around to Sandakan on the east coast of Sabah. There were no roads in the Fourth and Fifth Divisions or the Interior Residency, and in Third Division, there was only the coast road, which was some 150 miles from the border. Mapping was generally poor, as British maps of the country only showed tiny topographic detail. Indonesian maps were worse; veterans recall "a single black and white sheet for all of Kalimantan torn from a school textbook" in 1964.[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEConboy2003102-22)

Kalimantan was divided into four provinces, of which [East Kalimantan](/wiki/East_Kalimantan "East Kalimantan") and [West Kalimantan](/wiki/West_Kalimantan "West Kalimantan") bordered British Borneo. The capital of the West Kalimantan is [Pontianak](/wiki/Pontianak "Pontianak") on the west coast, about 100 miles (160 km) from the border, and the capital of East Kalimantan is [Samarinda](/wiki/Samarinda "Samarinda") on the south coast, some 350 kilometres (220 mi) from the border. There were no roads in the border area other than some in the west, and no road existed linking East and West Kalimantan.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The lack of roads and tracks suitable for vehicles on both sides of the border meant that movement was limited to foot tracks mostly unmarked on any map, as well as water and air movement. There were many large rivers on both sides of the border, and these were the primary means of movement. There were also quite a few small grass airstrips suitable for light aircraft, as dropping zones for parachuted supplies, and helicopters.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The equator lies about 160 km (100 mi) south of [Kuching](/wiki/Kuching "Kuching"), and most of northern Borneo receives over 3,000 mm (120 in) of rain each year. Borneo is naturally covered by tropical rainforests. This covers the mountainous areas cut by many rivers with very steep-sided hills and hilltop ridges often only a few metres wide. The high rainfall means large rivers; these provide a principal means of transport and are formidable tactical obstacles. Dense [mangrove forest](/wiki/Mangrove "Mangrove") covering vast tidal flats intersected with numerous creeks is a feature of many coastal areas, including Brunei and either end of the border. There are cultivated areas in valleys and around villages. In the vicinity of abandoned and current settlements are areas of dense secondary regrowth. [*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Sarawakian opposition

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indonesia%E2%80%93Malaysia_confrontation&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Sarawakian opposition")]

In 1946 the [Raj of Sarawak](/wiki/Raj_of_Sarawak "Raj of Sarawak"), [Charles Vyner Brooke](/wiki/Charles_Vyner_Brooke "Charles Vyner Brooke") ceded the state to the British Crown believing it to be in the best interest of the people of Sarawak following the end of [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II").[[21]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEReece199372-23) Sarawak became a Crown colony, ruled from the [Colonial Office](/wiki/Colonial_Office "Colonial Office") in [London](/wiki/London "London"), which in turn dispatched a [governor](/wiki/Governor "Governor") for Sarawak. The predominantly [Malay](/wiki/Malays_(ethnic_group) "Malays (ethnic group)") anti-cession movement, which rejected the British takeover of Sarawak and had assassinated [Duncan Stewart](/wiki/Duncan_Stewart_(colonial_administrator) "Duncan Stewart (colonial administrator)"), the first British [High Commissioner](/wiki/High_Commissioner "High Commissioner") of Sarawak, may have been the forerunner of the subsequent anti-Malaysia movement in Sarawak, headed by [Ahmad Zaidi Adruce](/wiki/Ahmad_Zaidi_Adruce "Ahmad Zaidi Adruce").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

According to Vernon L. Porritt and Hong-Kah Fong, [left-wing](/wiki/Left-wing_politics "Left-wing politics") and [communist](/wiki/Communism "Communism") [cells](/wiki/Clandestine_cell_system "Clandestine cell system") had been present among Sarawak's urban [Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_people "Chinese people") communities since the 1930s and 1940s. Some of the earliest Communist groups in Sabah included the Anti-Fascist League, which later became the Races Liberation Army, and the Borneo Anti-Japanese League, which was made up of the North Borneo Anti-Japanese League and the West Borneo Anti-Japanese League. The latter was led by Wu Chan, who was deported by the Sarawak colonial government to China in 1952. Other Communist groups in Sarawak included the Overseas Chinese Youth Association, which was formed in 1946, and the Liberation League along with its youth wing, the Advanced Youth Association, which emerged during the 1950s. These organisations became the nuclei for two Communist guerrilla movements: the anti-Malaysia North Kalimantan People's Army (PARAKU) and the Sarawak People's Guerrillas (PGRS). These various Communist groups were designated by various British and other Western sources as the [Clandestine Communist Organisation](/wiki/North_Kalimantan_Communist_Party "North Kalimantan Communist Party") (CCO) or the Sarawak Communist Organisation (SCO).[[22]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFong2005183–192-24)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e1/Members_of_the_SPGF%2C_NKNA_and_TNI_taking_picture_together.jpg/250px-Members_of_the_SPGF%2C_NKNA_and_TNI_taking_picture_together.jpg)](/wiki/File:Members_of_the_SPGF,_NKNA_and_TNI_taking_picture_together.jpg)

Members of the Sarawak People's Guerrilla Force (SPGF), North Kalimantan National Army (NKNA) and [Indonesian National Armed Forces](/wiki/Indonesian_National_Armed_Forces "Indonesian National Armed Forces") (TNI) taking photograph together marking the close relations between them during Indonesia under the rule of [Sukarno](/wiki/Sukarno "Sukarno").

The SCO was predominantly dominated by [ethnic Chinese](/wiki/Malaysian_Chinese "Malaysian Chinese") but also included [Dayak](/wiki/Dayak_people "Dayak people") supporters. However, the SCO had little support from ethnic Malays and other indigenous Sarawak peoples. At its height, the SCO had 24,000 members.[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECorbett1986124-7) During the 1940s and 1950s, [Maoism](/wiki/Maoism "Maoism") had spread among Chinese vernacular schools in Sarawak. Following World War II, Communist influence also penetrated the labour movement and the predominantly Chinese [Sarawak United People's Party](/wiki/Sarawak_United_People%27s_Party "Sarawak United People's Party"), the state's first political party, which was founded in June 1959. The Sarawak Insurgency began after the Brunei Revolt in 1962 and the SCO would fight alongside the [Bruneian rebels](/wiki/Brunei_People%27s_Party "Brunei People's Party") and Indonesian forces during the Indonesia–Malaysia confrontation.[[22]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFong2005183–192-24)[[23]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKheng2009132–152-25)

The SCO and the Bruneian rebels supported and propagated the unification of all British Borneo territories to form an independent leftist North Kalimantan state. This idea was originally proposed by [A. M. Azahari](/wiki/A._M._Azahari "A. M. Azahari"), leader of the Parti Rakyat Brunei (Brunei People's Party), who had forged links with [Sukarno](/wiki/Sukarno "Sukarno")'s nationalist movement, together with Ahmad Zaidi, in [Java](/wiki/Java "Java") in the 1940s. However, the Brunei People's Party was in favour of joining Malaysia on the condition it was unified with the three territories of northern Borneo with their own sultan, and hence was strong enough to resist domination by Malaya, Singapore, Malay administrators or Chinese merchants.[[4]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPocock1973129-6)

The [North Kalimantan](/wiki/North_Borneo_Federation "North Borneo Federation") (or Kalimantan Utara) proposal was seen as a post-[decolonisation](/wiki/Decolonisation "Decolonisation") alternative by local opposition against the Malaysia plan. Local opposition throughout the Borneo territories was primarily based on economic, political, historical and cultural differences between the Borneo states and Malaya, as well as the refusal to be subjected under peninsular political domination. Both Azahari and Zaidi went into exile in Indonesia during the confrontation. While the latter returned to Sarawak and had his political status rehabilitated, Azahari remained in Indonesia until his death on 3 September 2002.

In the aftermath of the Brunei Revolt, the remnants of the TNKU reached Indonesia. Possibly fearing British reprisals (which never came), many Chinese communists, possibly several thousand, also fled Sarawak. Their compatriots remaining in Sarawak were known as the CCO by the UK but called the PGRS—Pasukan Gelilya Rakyat Sarawak (Sarawak People's Guerrilla Force) by Indonesia. Soebandrio met with a group of their potential leaders in Bogor, and Nasution sent three trainers from Resimen Para Komando Angkatan Darat (RPKAD) Battalion 2 to Nangabadan near the Sarawak border, where there were about 300 trainees. Some three months later, two lieutenants were sent there.[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEConboy200393–95-3) The PGRS numbered about 800, based in West Kalimantan at Batu Hitam, with a contingent of 120 from the Indonesian intelligence agency and a small cadre trained in China. The PKI (Indonesian Communist Party) was firmly in evidence and led by an ethnic Arab revolutionary, Sofyan. The PGRS ran some raids into Sarawak but spent more time developing their supporters in Sarawak. The Indonesian military did not approve of the leftist nature of the PGRS and generally avoided them.[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEConboy2003156-4)

## Conflict

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indonesia%E2%80%93Malaysia_confrontation&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Conflict")]

### Beginning of hostilities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indonesia%E2%80%93Malaysia_confrontation&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Beginning of hostilities")]

Main articles: [Combat operations in 1963 during the Indonesian-Malaysian Confrontation](/wiki/Combat_operations_in_1963_during_the_Indonesian-Malaysian_Confrontation "Combat operations in 1963 during the Indonesian-Malaysian Confrontation"), [Combat operations in 1964 during the Indonesian-Malaysian Confrontation](/wiki/Combat_operations_in_1964_during_the_Indonesian-Malaysian_Confrontation "Combat operations in 1964 during the Indonesian-Malaysian Confrontation"), [Combat operations in 1965 during the Indonesian-Malaysian Confrontation](/wiki/Combat_operations_in_1965_during_the_Indonesian-Malaysian_Confrontation "Combat operations in 1965 during the Indonesian-Malaysian Confrontation"), and [Military history of Australia during the Indonesia–Malaysia confrontation](/wiki/Military_history_of_Australia_during_the_Indonesia%E2%80%93Malaysia_confrontation "Military history of Australia during the Indonesia–Malaysia confrontation")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c5/Soekarno.jpg/250px-Soekarno.jpg)](/wiki/File:Soekarno.jpg)

[Sukarno](/wiki/Sukarno "Sukarno")



Extracts from President Sukarno's Speech at the Indonesian Revolution Anniversary in 1963

Sukarno's motives for beginning the confrontation are contested. Former Indonesian Foreign Minister [Ide Anak Agung Gde Agung](/wiki/Ide_Anak_Agung_Gde_Agung "Ide Anak Agung Gde Agung") argued years later that Sukarno intentionally muted Indonesia's opposition to the proposed Malaysian state while Indonesia was preoccupied with advancing its claim to [West New Guinea](/wiki/Dutch_New_Guinea "Dutch New Guinea"). Following Indonesia's diplomatic victory in the West New Guinea dispute, Sukarno may have been emboldened to extend Indonesia's dominance over its weaker neighbours. Conversely, Sukarno may have felt compelled by the ongoing pressure of the PKI and the general instability of Indonesian politics to divert attention towards a new foreign conflict.

In the late 1950s, Sukarno argued that Malaysia was a British puppet state, a neo-colonial experiment and that any expansion of Malaysia would increase British control over the region, with implications for Indonesia's national security. Sukarno strongly opposed the British decolonisation initiative involving the formation of the Federation of Malaysia that would comprise the Malay Peninsula and British Borneo. Sukarno accused Malaysia of being a British puppet state aimed at establishing neo-imperialism and neo-colonialism in Southeast Asia, and also at containing Indonesian ambition to be the regional hegemonic power.[[24]](#cite_note-HirakawaShimizu1999-26)

It was also suggested that Sukarno's campaign against the formation of Malaysia was motivated by a desire to separate Malaya, North Borneo, Sarawak, and Singapore as a separate country, hence not submitting to the British proposal for [decolonization](/wiki/Decolonization "Decolonization") calling it as [neocolonialism](/wiki/Neocolonialism "Neocolonialism") done by the British state as a way of spreading British hegemony in the region.[[25]](#cite_note-27)[[26]](#cite_note-28) Similarly, the [Philippines](/wiki/Philippines "Philippines") claimed eastern North Borneo, arguing that the Borneo colony had historical links with the Philippines through the [Sultanate of Sulu](/wiki/Sultanate_of_Sulu "Sultanate of Sulu").
However, while Sukarno made no direct claims to incorporate northern Borneo into Indonesian Kalimantan, he saw the formation of Malaysia as an obstacle to the [Maphilindo](/wiki/Maphilindo "Maphilindo"), a non-political, [irredentist](/wiki/Irredentism "Irredentism") union spanning Malaya, Philippines and Indonesia.[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPocock1973113-29) [President of the Philippines](/wiki/President_of_the_Philippines "President of the Philippines") [Diosdado Macapagal](/wiki/Diosdado_Macapagal "Diosdado Macapagal") initially did not oppose the concept and even initiated the [Manila Accord](/wiki/Manila_Accord "Manila Accord"). While the Philippines did not engage in hostilities, it did defer recognising Malaysia as the [successor state](/wiki/Successor_state "Successor state") to Malaya. Consequently, Malaysia severed diplomatic ties with the Philippines. Indonesia argued that the establishment of Malaysia allowed the United Kingdom to maintain their unique privileges regarding the use of the Singapore base and keep close ties to British defense needs in Southeast Asia constituted an implied threat. [Subandrio](/wiki/Subandrio "Subandrio"), the Indonesian foreign minister, was careful to explain to American ambassador [Howard P. Jones](/wiki/Howard_P._Jones "Howard P. Jones") that the confrontation policy was concerned with Malaya not Malaysia and was a reaction to Malayan and British anti-Djakarta, pro-rebel activity in 1958, and promotion of program to split off Sumatra as diplomatic efforts to settle the Malaysian dispute picked up steam in the summer of 1963 through ministerial and summit level talks in Manila.[[18]](#cite_note-EnglishHistoricalReview-Nov99-20)

In April 1963, the first recorded infiltration and attack occurred in Borneo. An infiltration force training at Nangabadan was split in two and prepared for its first operation. On 12 April 1963, one infiltration force attacked and seized the police station at [Tebedu](/wiki/Tebedu "Tebedu") in the 1st Division of Sarawak, about 64 km (40 miles) from Kuching and 3.2 km (2 miles) from the border with Kalimantan.[[28]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPocock1973153-30) The other group attacked the village of Gumbang, South West of Kuching, later in the month. Only about half returned.[[29]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEConboy200395-31) The confrontation could be said to have started from a military perspective with the Tebedu attack.[[30]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDennis_et_al.2008152-32)

Before Indonesia's declaration of confrontation against the proposed Malaysian state on 20 January 1963, the [Cobbold Commission](/wiki/Cobbold_Commission "Cobbold Commission") in 1962 had reported on the viability of a Malaysian state, finding that there was sufficient support in the Borneo colonies for the creation of a larger Malaysian state. However, due to hardening Indonesian and Philippine opposition to the Malaysia proposal, a new round of negotiations was proposed to hear the Indonesian and Philippine points of opposition. To resolve the dispute the would-be member states of Malaysia met representatives of Indonesia and the Philippines in [Manila](/wiki/Manila "Manila") for several days, starting on 30 July 1963. Just days before the summit, on 27 July 1963, Sukarno had continued his inflammatory rhetoric, declaring that he was going to "crush Malaysia" ([Indonesian](/wiki/Indonesian_language "Indonesian language"): *Ganyang Malaysia*). At the Manila meeting, the Philippines and Indonesia formally agreed to accept the formation of Malaysia if a majority in North Borneo and Sarawak voted for it in a referendum organised by the [United Nations](/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations"). While the fact-finding mission by the UN was expected to begin on 22 August, Indonesian delaying tactics forced the mission to start on 26 August. Nevertheless, the UN expected the report to be published by 14 September 1963.[[31]](#cite_note-Tun_Hanif_Omar_2007-33)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5a/Tunku_abd_rahman.jpg/250px-Tunku_abd_rahman.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tunku_abd_rahman.jpg)

[Tunku Abdul Rahman](/wiki/Tunku_Abdul_Rahman "Tunku Abdul Rahman")

Before the Manila meeting, the Malayan Government had set 31 August as the date on which Malaysia would come into existence (coinciding with Malaya's [independence day celebrations of 31 August](/wiki/Independence_Day_(Malaysia) "Independence Day (Malaysia)")). However, at the Manila negotiations, it was persuaded by the Indonesian and Philippine Governments to postpone Malaysia's inauguration until 15 September 1963 by which time a UN mission was expected to report on whether the two Borneo colonies supported the Malaysia proposal.[[32]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEdwards1992260-34) However, following the conclusion of the Manila talks, the Malayan prime minister [Tunku Abdul Rahman](/wiki/Tunku_Abdul_Rahman "Tunku Abdul Rahman") announced that the proposed Malaysian state would come into existence on 16 September 1963, apparently irrespective of the latest UN report.[[33]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMackie1974174–175-35)

North Borneo and Sarawak, anticipating a pro-Malaysian UN report, declared their independence as part of Malaysia on the sixth anniversary of Malayan independence, 31 August 1963, before the UN report had been published.[[31]](#cite_note-Tun_Hanif_Omar_2007-33) On 14 September the UN report was published, once again providing general endorsement of the proposed Malaysian state. [Malaysia](/wiki/Malaysia "Malaysia") was formally established on 16 September 1963. Indonesia immediately reacted by expelling the Malaysian Ambassador from [Jakarta](/wiki/Jakarta "Jakarta"). Two days later, rioters organised by the PKI burned the British embassy in Jakarta. Several hundred rioters ransacked the Singaporean embassy in Jakarta and the homes of Singaporean diplomats. In Malaysia, Indonesian agents were captured, and crowds attacked the Indonesian embassy in [Kuala Lumpur](/wiki/Kuala_Lumpur "Kuala Lumpur").[[34]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPocock1973173-36)

### Infiltration campaign

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indonesia%E2%80%93Malaysia_confrontation&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Infiltration campaign")]

Even as peace talks progressed and stalled, Indonesia maintained its campaign of infiltrations. On 14 August, a headman reported an incursion in the 3rd Division and a follow-up indicated they were about 50 strong. A series of contacts ensued as [2/6 Gurkhas](/wiki/6th_Queen_Elizabeth%27s_Own_Gurkha_Rifles "6th Queen Elizabeth's Own Gurkha Rifles") deployed patrols and ambushes, and after a month, 15 had been killed and three captured. The Gurkhas reported that they were well trained and professionally led, but their ammunition expenditure was high, and their fire discipline broke down. The prisoners reported 300 more invaders within a week and 600 in a fortnight.[[35]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPocock1973170-37) The [Battle of Long Jawai](/wiki/Battle_of_Long_Jawai "Battle of Long Jawai") was the first major incursion for the centre of the 3rd Division, directed by an RPKAD Major Mulyono Soerjowardojo,[[36]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEvan_der_Bijl200780–85-38) who had been sent to Nangabadan earlier in the year.[[29]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEConboy200395-31) The proclamation of Malaysia in September 1963 meant that Malaysian Army units deployed former British Borneo now known as East Malaysia.[[37]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMajid2007154-39)

The deliberate attack by Indonesian forces on Malaysian troops did not enhance Sukarno's "anti-imperialist" credentials, although the Indonesian government tried blaming their [Navy Commando Corps](/wiki/Indonesian_Marine_Corps "Indonesian Marine Corps") (*Korps Komando*, KKO) as enthusiastic idealists acting independently. They also produced Azahari, who claimed that Indonesian forces were playing no part in active operations. Sukarno next launched a peace offensive and, in late January, declared he was ready for a ceasefire (despite having denied direct Indonesia involvement). Talks started in Bangkok, but border violations continued, and the talks soon failed. They resumed mid-year in Tokyo and failed within days but allowed time for a Thai mission to visit Sarawak and witness, well-equipped Indonesian soldiers withdrawing across the border, which they had crossed a short distance away earlier in the day.[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPocock1973179–181,_188-40)

Meanwhile, the Indonesian armed forces led by Lieutenant General [Ahmad Yani](/wiki/Ahmad_Yani "Ahmad Yani") became increasingly concerned with the worsening domestic situation in Indonesia and began secretly contacting the Malaysian government, while obstructing the confrontation, reducing it to a minimal level.[[39]](#cite_note-41) This was implemented to preserve an already exhausted army which had recently conducted [Operation Trikora](/wiki/Operation_Trikora "Operation Trikora") in Western New Guinea, while also maintaining its political position in Indonesian politics, especially against the Communist Party of Indonesia, the ardent supporters of the confrontation.[[40]](#cite_note-42)

### Expansion of the conflict to the Malaysian Peninsula

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indonesia%E2%80%93Malaysia_confrontation&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Expansion of the conflict to the Malaysian Peninsula")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/84/Malaysian_Rangers%2C_Malay-Thai_border_%28AWM_MAL-65-0046-01%29.JPG/250px-Malaysian_Rangers%2C_Malay-Thai_border_%28AWM_MAL-65-0046-01%29.JPG)](/wiki/File:Malaysian_Rangers,_Malay-Thai_border_(AWM_MAL-65-0046-01).JPG)

[Sarawak Rangers](/wiki/Sarawak_Rangers "Sarawak Rangers") (subsequently part of Malaysian Rangers) comprising [Ibans](/wiki/Iban_people "Iban people") leap from a [Royal Australian Air Force](/wiki/Royal_Australian_Air_Force "Royal Australian Air Force") [Bell UH-1 Iroquois](/wiki/Bell_UH-1_Iroquois "Bell UH-1 Iroquois") helicopter to guard the [Malay–Thai](/wiki/Malaysia%E2%80%93Thailand_border "Malaysia–Thailand border") border.

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/87/Gnome-mime-sound-openclipart.svg/60px-Gnome-mime-sound-openclipart.svg.png)

[Sukarno's address](/wiki/File:President_Sukarno_announces_Dwikomando_Rakyat.ogg "File:President Sukarno announces Dwikomando Rakyat.ogg")

President [Sukarno](/wiki/Sukarno "Sukarno") announcing the *Dwi Komando Rakyat*, 3 May 1964.

---

*Problems playing this file? See [media help](/wiki/Help:Media "Help:Media").*

On 3 May 1964, amid ongoing hostilities, Sukarno proclaimed the People's Dual Command or *Dwi Komando Rakyat* (Dwikora). The Dwikora contained Sukarno's call to defend the Indonesian Revolution and support revolutions in Malaya, Singapore, Sarawak and Sabah to destroy Malaysia. Co-ordinated to coincide with Sukarno's announcement of the ['Year of Living Dangerously'](/wiki/Vivere_pericoloso "Vivere pericoloso") during Indonesian Independence Day celebrations, Indonesian forces began a campaign of airborne and seaborne infiltrations of the Malaysian Peninsula on 17 August 1964. On 17 August 1964, a seaborne force of about 100, composed of air force [Rapid Response Troop](/wiki/Kopasgat#PGT_Regimental_Combat_Team_(RTP-PGT) "Kopasgat") (*Pasukan Gerak Tjepat*, PGT) paratroopers, KKO and about a dozen Malaysian communists, crossed the [Strait of Malacca](/wiki/Strait_of_Malacca "Strait of Malacca") by boat, [landing at Pontian](/wiki/Landing_at_Pontian "Landing at Pontian") in three parties in the night.[[41]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEConboy2003161-43) Instead of being greeted as liberators, however, they were contained by various Commonwealth forces, and all but four of the infiltrators were captured within a few days.[[42]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJamesSheil-Small1971146-44)

On 2 September, three [Lockheed C-130 Hercules](/wiki/Lockheed_C-130_Hercules "Lockheed C-130 Hercules") aircraft set off from Jakarta for [Peninsular Malaysia](/wiki/Peninsular_Malaysia "Peninsular Malaysia"), flying low to avoid detection by radar. The following night, two of the C-130 reached their objective with their onboard PGT paratroopers, who jumped off and landed around [Labis](/wiki/Landing_at_Labis "Landing at Labis") in Johor (about 160 kilometres (100 miles) north of Singapore). The remaining C-130 crashed into the Strait of Malacca while trying to evade interception by an RAF [Javelin FAW 9](/wiki/Gloster_Javelin "Gloster Javelin") launched from [RAF Tengah](/wiki/RAF_Tengah "RAF Tengah").[[41]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEConboy2003161-43) Due to a lightning storm, the drop of 96 paratroopers was widely dispersed. This resulted in them landing close to 1/10 Gurkhas, who were joined by 1st Battalion, [Royal New Zealand Infantry Regiment](/wiki/Royal_New_Zealand_Infantry_Regiment "Royal New Zealand Infantry Regiment") (1 RNZIR) stationed near Malacca with [28 (Commonwealth) Brigade](/wiki/28th_Infantry_Brigade_(United_Kingdom) "28th Infantry Brigade (United Kingdom)"). Operations were commanded by four Malaysian Brigade, but it took a month for the security forces to capture or kill 90 of the 96 paratroopers, with two men killed during the action.[[43]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEvan_der_Bijl2007135–138-45)[[44]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJamesSheil-Small1971148–150-46)[[45]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPugsley2003206–213-47)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/89/Proclamation_of_Emergency-Indonesia_%26_Malaysia_confrontation_1964_%28L.N._271_of_1964%29_%28cropped%29.png/250px-Proclamation_of_Emergency-Indonesia_%26_Malaysia_confrontation_1964_%28L.N._271_of_1964%29_%28cropped%29.png)](/wiki/File:Proclamation_of_Emergency-Indonesia_%26_Malaysia_confrontation_1964_(L.N._271_of_1964)_(cropped).png)

Emergency proclamation issued by the Yang di-Pertuan Agong on 3 September 1964.

On 3 September, following the Labis attack, the [Yang di-Pertuan Agong](/wiki/King_of_Malaysia "King of Malaysia") of Malaysia, on the advice of the [Malaysian cabinet](/wiki/Cabinet_of_Malaysia "Cabinet of Malaysia"), declared a [state of emergency](/wiki/State_of_emergency "State of emergency") throughout the country, with the intention to enable speedy trial and invoke the death penalty for captured invaders.[[46]](#cite_note-48)[[47]](#cite_note-49) The emergency proclamation was endorsed by the [House of Representatives](/wiki/Dewan_Rakyat "Dewan Rakyat") and [Senate](/wiki/Dewan_Negara "Dewan Negara") of the [Malaysian parliament](/wiki/Parliament_of_Malaysia "Parliament of Malaysia") on 10 September[[48]](#cite_note-50)[[49]](#cite_note-51)[[50]](#cite_note-:0-52) and 11 September[[51]](#cite_note-53)[[52]](#cite_note-:1-54) respectively, which was immediately followed by the passage of the Emergency (Essential Powers) Act 1964 in the two chambers.[[50]](#cite_note-:0-52)[[52]](#cite_note-:1-54)

Meanwhile, Indonesia's expansion of the conflict to the Malaysian Peninsula sparked the [Sunda Straits Crisis](/wiki/Sunda_Straits_Crisis "Sunda Straits Crisis"), involving the anticipated transit of the Sunda Strait by the British aircraft carrier [HMS *Victorious*](/wiki/HMS_Victorious_(R38) "HMS Victorious (R38)") and two destroyer escorts. Commonwealth forces were readied for airstrikes against Indonesian infiltration staging areas in Sumatra if further Indonesian infiltrations of the Malaysian Peninsula were attempted. A tense three-week standoff occurred before the crisis was peacefully resolved.[[53]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEdwards1992319-55)

By the final months of 1964, the conflict once again appeared to have reached a stalemate, with Commonwealth forces having placed Indonesia's campaign of infiltrations into East Malaysia in check for the moment, and more recently, the Malaysian Peninsula.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] However, the fragile equilibrium looked likely to change once again in December 1964 when Commonwealth intelligence began reporting a build-up of Indonesian infiltration forces in Kalimantan opposite Kuching, which suggested the possibility of an escalation in hostilities. Two additional British battalions were subsequently deployed to Borneo.[[54]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGregorian199155-56)[[55]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJones2002272-57) Meanwhile, due to the landings in Malaysia and Indonesia's continued troop build-up, Australia and New Zealand also agreed to begin deploying combat forces to Borneo in early 1965.[[56]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEvan_der_Bijl2007165-58)

### Operation Claret

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indonesia%E2%80%93Malaysia_confrontation&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Operation Claret")]

Main article: [Operation Claret](/wiki/Operation_Claret "Operation Claret")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/68/Queen%27s_Own_Highlanders_searching_for_enemies_during_a_patrol.jpg/250px-Queen%27s_Own_Highlanders_searching_for_enemies_during_a_patrol.jpg)](/wiki/File:Queen%27s_Own_Highlanders_searching_for_enemies_during_a_patrol.jpg)

[Queen's Own Highlanders](/wiki/Queen%27s_Own_Highlanders_(Seaforth_and_Camerons) "Queen's Own Highlanders (Seaforth and Camerons)") 1st Battalion conduct a patrol to search for enemy positions in the jungle of Brunei.

Operation Claret was a long-running series of secretive cross-border raids conducted by Commonwealth forces in Borneo from June 1964 to early 1966. These raids were undertaken by [special forces](/wiki/Special_forces "Special forces")—including the British [Special Air Service](/wiki/Special_Air_Service "Special Air Service"), Australian [Special Air Service Regiment](/wiki/Special_Air_Service_Regiment "Special Air Service Regiment"), and [New Zealand Special Air Service](/wiki/New_Zealand_Special_Air_Service "New Zealand Special Air Service")—as well as regular [infantry](/wiki/Infantry "Infantry"). During the early phases of the conflict, Commonwealth and Malaysian troops had attempted only to control the border and to protect population centres from Indonesian attacks. However, by 1965 they had decided to take more aggressive action, crossing the border to obtain information and in "hot pursuit" of withdrawing Indonesian infiltrators.[[30]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDennis_et_al.2008152-32) First approved in May 1965, and expanded to include cross-border ambushing in July.[[57]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPugsley2003255-59)

These patrols—which were highly classified at the time—often involved small reconnaissance teams crossing the border from the Malaysian states of Sarawak or Sabah into Indonesian Kalimantan in order to detect Indonesian forces about to enter East Malaysia. Initially, penetration was limited to 2,700 metres (3,000 yards) but was later extended to 5,500 metres (6,000 yards), and again to 9,100 metres (10,000 yards) after the [Battle of Plaman Mapu](/wiki/Battle_of_Plaman_Mapu "Battle of Plaman Mapu") in April 1965.[[58]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESmith199941-60)[[59]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDennisGrey1996232–233-61) Conventional follow-up forces of [platoon](/wiki/Platoon "Platoon") and [company](/wiki/Company_(military_unit) "Company (military unit)") size were then directed into position to ambush the Indonesians, either as they crossed the border or often while they were still in Kalimantan. Such operations were to be "deniable" and were conducted under a policy of "aggressive defence".[[57]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPugsley2003255-59) Given the sensitivity of these operations and the potential consequences if they were exposed, they were controlled at the highest level and conducted within strict parameters known as the "Golden Rules", while the participants were sworn to secrecy.[[60]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHorner200283–84-62)

Claret was largely successful in gaining the initiative for the Commonwealth forces before being suspended late in the war, inflicting significant casualties on the Indonesians and keeping them on the defensive on their side of the border.[[57]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPugsley2003255-59)[[61]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDennisGrey1996307-63)

## Counter-measures

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indonesia%E2%80%93Malaysia_confrontation&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Counter-measures")]

### Command arrangements

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indonesia%E2%80%93Malaysia_confrontation&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Command arrangements")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/22/Malaysian_Border_Scouts_comprising_indigenous_peoples_of_Borneo_.jpg/250px-Malaysian_Border_Scouts_comprising_indigenous_peoples_of_Borneo_.jpg)](/wiki/File:Malaysian_Border_Scouts_comprising_indigenous_peoples_of_Borneo_.jpg)

Some 1,500 men from the indigenous tribes of Sabah and Sarawak were recruited by the [Malaysian government](/wiki/Government_of_Malaysia "Government of Malaysia") as Border Scouts under the command of Richard Noone and other officers from the [Senoi Praaq](/wiki/Senoi_Praaq "Senoi Praaq") to counter the Indonesian infiltrations.

In early January 1963, the military forces in northern Borneo, having arrived in December 1962 in response to the Brunei Revolt, were under the command of Commander British Forces Borneo (COMBRITBOR), Major General [Walter Walker](/wiki/Walter_Walker_(British_Army_officer) "Walter Walker (British Army officer)"), who was Director of Borneo Operations (DOBOPS) based on [Labuan](/wiki/Labuan "Labuan") Island and reported directly to the Commander in Chief Far East Forces, Admiral Sir [David Luce](/wiki/David_Luce "David Luce").[[62]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDennisGrey1996239–240-64) Luce was routinely replaced by Admiral Sir [Varyl Begg](/wiki/Varyl_Begg "Varyl Begg") in early 1963.[[63]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJamesSheil-Small197160-65) In the middle of 1963, Brigadier Pat Glennie, normally the Brigadier General Staff in Singapore, arrived as Deputy DOBOPS.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Politico-military authority lay with the Emergency Committees in Sarawak and North Borneo, including their governors, who were the Commanders in Chief for their colonies. In Brunei, there was a State Advisory Council answerable to the Sultan.[[64]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMockaitis199529-66) After independence, supreme authority changed to the Malaysian National Defence Council in Kuala Lumpur with State Executive Committees in Sabah and Sarawak. Military direction was from the Malaysian National Operations Committee jointly chaired by the Chief of the Malaysian Armed Forces Staff, General [Tunku Osman](/wiki/Tunku_Osman "Tunku Osman"), and the Inspector General of Police, Sir Claude Fenner. The British Commander in Chief Far East Forces was a member. DOBOPS regularly attended its meetings.[[65]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEvan_der_Bijl200775-67)

### Commonwealth order of battle

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indonesia%E2%80%93Malaysia_confrontation&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Commonwealth order of battle")]

Main article: [Order of battle during Indonesia-Malaysia confrontation](/wiki/Order_of_battle_during_Indonesia-Malaysia_confrontation "Order of battle during Indonesia-Malaysia confrontation")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/48/Australian_soldiers_ferried_in_small_craft_to_guard_Sabah_%28AWM_NAVY05185%29.JPG/250px-Australian_soldiers_ferried_in_small_craft_to_guard_Sabah_%28AWM_NAVY05185%29.JPG)](/wiki/File:Australian_soldiers_ferried_in_small_craft_to_guard_Sabah_(AWM_NAVY05185).JPG)

Australian soldiers being ferried in a small craft, from troop transport [HMAS *Sydney*](/wiki/HMAS_Sydney_(R17) "HMAS Sydney (R17)") on its arrival in North Borneo (Sabah) as part of their defence aid programme to Malaysia.

British forces in Borneo included Headquarters (HQ) 3 Commando Brigade in Kuching with responsibility for the western part of Sarawak, 1st, 2nd and 3rd Divisions, and HQ 99 Gurkha Infantry Brigade in Brunei responsible for the East, 4th and 5th Divisions, Brunei and Sabah.[[66]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEvan_der_Bijl200739-68) These HQs had deployed from Singapore in late 1962 in response to the Brunei Revolt. The ground forces were initially limited to just five British and Gurkha infantry battalions usually based in Malaya, Singapore and Hong Kong,[[67]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGregorian199149-69) and an armoured car squadron.[[68]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEvan_der_Bijl200734-70) The police also deployed several light infantry of [Police Field Force](/wiki/General_Operations_Force "General Operations Force") companies.[[69]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFrench201117-71) However, as additional resources became available the size of the force available to Walker expanded, and by the end of 1964 British forces had grown to approximately 14,000 troops organised into three brigades (increased to four in 1965).[[8]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarver1986806-10) The naval effort, under DOBOPS command, was primarily provided by minesweepers used to patrol coastal waters and larger inland waterways around Wallace Bay. A [guardship](/wiki/Guardship "Guardship") – a frigate or destroyer – was stationed off Tawau.[[70]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGrey199857–58-72)

Before the confrontation, no British military units had been stationed in Sabah or Sarawak. As the conflict developed increasing numbers of troops were required. There were three types of British Army deployment: Units stationed in the Far East for two years did a single 4-month tour (this applied to Australian and New Zealander forces); Gurkha units (all permanently stationed in the Far East) did 6 month tours, generally once every twelve months; UK based units (from [Army Strategic Command](/wiki/Army_Strategic_Command_(United_Kingdom) "Army Strategic Command (United Kingdom)")) did 12 month tours including 6 weeks jungle warfare training in Peninsular Malaysia.

The initial air component based in Borneo consisted of detachments from squadrons stationed in Malaya and Singapore. These included [Twin Pioneer](/wiki/Scottish_Aviation_Twin_Pioneer "Scottish Aviation Twin Pioneer") and [Single Pioneer](/wiki/Scottish_Aviation_Pioneer "Scottish Aviation Pioneer") transport aircraft, probably two or three [Blackburn Beverley](/wiki/Blackburn_Beverley "Blackburn Beverley") and [Handley Page Hastings](/wiki/Handley_Page_Hastings "Handley Page Hastings") transports, and about 12 helicopters of various types. One of Walker's first "challenges" was curtailing the RAF's centralised command and control arrangements and insisting that aircraft tasking for operations in Borneo was by his HQ, not by the [RAF Air Command Far East](/wiki/Far_East_Air_Force_(Royal_Air_Force) "Far East Air Force (Royal Air Force)") HQ in Singapore.[[71]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPocock1973159–160-73) Other aircraft of many types stationed in Malaya and Singapore provided sorties as necessary, including routine transport support into Kuching and Labuan.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Rotary wing support included 60 naval and air force troop-lift helicopters and another 40 smaller army variants.[[8]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarver1986806-10)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e4/Captured_Indonesian_Infiltrators_%28AWM_P01499.005%29.JPG/250px-Captured_Indonesian_Infiltrators_%28AWM_P01499.005%29.JPG)](/wiki/File:Captured_Indonesian_Infiltrators_(AWM_P01499.005).JPG)

Captured Indonesian infiltrators near [Kesang River](/wiki/Kesang_River "Kesang River"), Terendak, [Malacca](/wiki/Malacca "Malacca") on 29 October 1964 by the [Royal Australian Regiment](/wiki/Royal_Australian_Regiment "Royal Australian Regiment").

Patrols within Malaysia were supplied while in the field by RN Wessex and RAF [Whirlwind](/wiki/Westland_Whirlwind_(helicopter) "Westland Whirlwind (helicopter)") helicopters, initially dropping supplies to the patrols from low level, and later after the patrols had cleared a landing area in the dense jungle, by landing. A test Joint Service deployment of a [Westland SR.N5](/wiki/SR.N5 "SR.N5") hovercraft at [Tawau](/wiki/Tawau "Tawau") was also trialled under Major [John Simpson](/wiki/John_Simpson_(British_Army_officer) "John Simpson (British Army officer)").

In addition to the ground and air force units, between 1963 and 1966 there were up to 80 ships from the Royal Navy, Royal Australian Navy, Royal Malay Navy, Royal New Zealand Navy and the Royal Fleet Auxiliary.[[72]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGrey199855-74) Most of these were patrol craft, minesweepers, frigates and destroyers patrolling the coast-line to intercept Indonesian insurgents. One of the two Commando Carriers, [HMS *Albion*](/wiki/HMS_Albion_(R07) "HMS Albion (R07)") and [HMS *Bulwark*](/wiki/HMS_Bulwark_(R08) "HMS Bulwark (R08)"), was also committed throughout the confrontation usually in their transport role for troops, helicopters and army aircraft between Singapore and Borneo.[[73]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPolmar2008192-75)

In the early stages of the conflict, Indonesian forces were under the command of Lieutenant General Zulkipli in Pontianak, on the coast of West Kalimantan about 200 km (120 mi) from the border. The Indonesian irregulars, led by Indonesian officers, were thought to number about 1,500, with an unknown number of regular troops and local defence irregulars. They were deployed the entire length of the border in eight operational units, mostly facing the 1st and 2nd Divisions. The units had names such as "Thunderbolts", "Night Ghosts" and "World Sweepers".[[74]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPocock1973168-76) However, as the conflict developed, the poorly trained and equipped 'volunteers' had been replaced by regular units.[[75]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDennisGrey1996204-77) Indonesian forces deployed along the border in Kalimantan increased significantly towards the end of 1964, with estimates of between 15,000 and 30,000 men, up from around 2,500 men in mid-1964.[[76]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMackie1974215-78)

### Intelligence

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indonesia%E2%80%93Malaysia_confrontation&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Intelligence")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/39/Murut_tribesman_consulting_the_map_of_Sabah_with_Australian_Engineer_%28AWM_CUN-65-0899A-MC%29.JPG/250px-Murut_tribesman_consulting_the_map_of_Sabah_with_Australian_Engineer_%28AWM_CUN-65-0899A-MC%29.JPG)](/wiki/File:Murut_tribesman_consulting_the_map_of_Sabah_with_Australian_Engineer_(AWM_CUN-65-0899A-MC).JPG)

A [Murut tribesman](/wiki/Murut_people "Murut people") consulting the terrain map of [Sabah](/wiki/Sabah "Sabah") (North Borneo) with [Royal Australian Engineers](/wiki/Royal_Australian_Engineers "Royal Australian Engineers") member.

A factor in the containment of the Indonesian forces was the use of [intelligence](/wiki/Intelligence_(information_gathering) "Intelligence (information gathering)"). The [Royal Signals](/wiki/Royal_Signals "Royal Signals") were able to intercept the Indonesian military communications. The [cyphers](/wiki/Cipher "Cipher") were decrypted by the [Intelligence Corps](/wiki/Intelligence_Corps_(United_Kingdom) "Intelligence Corps (United Kingdom)") based at [Government Communications Headquarters](/wiki/Government_Communications_Headquarters "Government Communications Headquarters") (GCHQ) listening stations in Singapore, one of which was [RAF Chia Keng](/wiki/RAF_Chia_Keng "RAF Chia Keng") which was linked directly to the RAF Far East Air Force headquarters at [RAF Changi](/wiki/RAF_Changi "RAF Changi"). Intelligence from this may have been used in planning some aspects of Claret cross-border operations.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### British tactics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indonesia%E2%80%93Malaysia_confrontation&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: British tactics")]

Soon after assuming command in Borneo, General Walker issued a directive listing the ingredients for success, based on his experience in the [Malayan Emergency](/wiki/Malayan_Emergency "Malayan Emergency"):

* Unified operations (army, navy and air force operating fully together)
* Timely and accurate information (the need for continuous reconnaissance and intelligence collection)
* Speed, mobility and flexibility
* Security of bases
* Domination of the jungle
* Winning the hearts and minds of the people (this was added several months later).[[77]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPimlott198495-79)

Walker recognised the difficulties of limited forces and a long border and, in early 1963, was reinforced with an SAS squadron from the UK, which rotated with another mid-year. When the SAS temporarily adopted 3-man instead of 4-man patrols, they could not closely monitor the border. Increasing the capability of the infantry to create a surveillance network was also considered essential.

Walker raised the Border Scouts, building on Harrison's force of [Kelabits](/wiki/Kelabit_people "Kelabit people"), who had mobilised to help intercept the fleeing TNKU forces from the Brunei Revolt. He also utilised the experience of the Royal Marines as well as knowledge and skill of the [Sarawak Rangers](/wiki/Sarawak_Rangers "Sarawak Rangers") who had served during the Malayan Emergency. This was approved by the Sarawak government in May as "auxiliary police". Walker selected Lieutenant Colonel John Cross, a Gurkha officer with immense jungle experience, for the task. A training centre was established in a remote area at Mount Murat in the 5th Division and staffed mainly by SAS. Border Scouts were attached to infantry battalions and evolved into an intelligence-gathering force by using their local knowledge and extended families.[[78]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPocock1973165–166-80) In addition, the Police Special Branch, which had proved so effective during the Malayan Emergency in recruiting sources in the communist organisation, was expanded.[[79]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPimlott198497-81)

British jungle tactics were developed and honed during the Malayan Emergency against a clever and elusive enemy. They emphasised travelling lightly, being undetectable and going for many days without resupplying. Being undetectable meant being silent (hand signals, no rattling equipment) and 'odour free'—perfumed toiletries were forbidden (they could be detected a kilometre away by good jungle fighters), and sometimes eating food cold to prevent cooking smells.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/70/Royal_Army_Medical_Corps_examines_a_baby.jpg/250px-Royal_Army_Medical_Corps_examines_a_baby.jpg)](/wiki/File:Royal_Army_Medical_Corps_examines_a_baby.jpg)

A [Royal Army Medical Corps](/wiki/Royal_Army_Medical_Corps "Royal Army Medical Corps") captain examines a Murut child whose parents have fled from [Indonesian Borneo](/wiki/Kalimantan "Kalimantan") to Sarawak, the British have [won the hearts of people](/wiki/Psychological_warfare "Psychological warfare") in the border.

In about 1962, at the end of [National Service](/wiki/National_Service "National Service"), British infantry battalions had reorganised into three rifle companies, a support company and an HQ company with logistic responsibilities. Battalion HQ included an intelligence section. Each rifle company was composed of 3 platoons of 32 men each, equipped with [light machine guns](/wiki/Bren_light_machine_gun "Bren light machine gun") and [self-loading rifles](/wiki/L1A1_Self-Loading_Rifle "L1A1 Self-Loading Rifle"). The support company had a mortar platoon with six medium mortars ([3-inch mortar](/wiki/Ordnance_ML_3_inch_Mortar "Ordnance ML 3 inch Mortar") until replaced by [81-mm mortar](/wiki/L16_81mm_Mortar "L16 81mm Mortar") around the end of 1965) organised into three sections, enabling a section to be attached to a rifle company if required. Similarly organised was an anti-tank platoon; there was also an [assault pioneer](/wiki/Assault_pioneer "Assault pioneer") platoon. The machine gun platoon was abolished, but the impending delivery of the [7.62 mm GPMG](/wiki/FN_MAG "FN MAG"), with sustained fire kits held by each company, was to provide a medium machine gun capability. In the meantime, the [Vickers machine gun](/wiki/Vickers_machine_gun "Vickers machine gun") remained available. The innovation in the new organisation was the formation of the battalion reconnaissance platoon,[[80]](#cite_note-82) in many battalions a platoon of "chosen men". In Borneo, mortars were usually distributed to rifle companies, and some battalions operated the rest of their support company as another rifle company.

The basic activity was platoon patrolling; this continued throughout the campaign, with patrols being deployed by helicopter, roping in and out as necessary. Movement was usually single file; the leading section rotated but was organised with two lead scouts, followed by its commander and then the remainder in a fire support group. Battle drills for "contact front" (or rear), or "ambush left" (or right) were highly developed. Poor maps meant navigation was important; however, the local knowledge of the Border Scouts in Borneo compensated for the poor maps. So tracks were sometimes used unless ambush was considered possible, or there was the possibility of mines. Crossing obstacles such as rivers was also handled as a battle drill. At night, a platoon harboured in a tight position with all-round defence.

Contact while moving was always possible. However, offensive action usually took two forms: either an attack on a camp or an ambush. The tactic for dealing with a camp was to get a party behind it then charge the front. However, ambushes were probably the most effective tactic and could be sustained for many days. They targeted tracks and, particularly in parts of Borneo, waterways. Track ambushes were close range, 10 to 20 m (11 to 22 yd), with a [killing zone](/wiki/Kill_zone "Kill zone") typically 20 to 50 m (22 to 55 yd) long, depending on the expected strength of the target. The trick was to remain undetected when the target entered the ambush area and then open fire all together.

Fire support was limited for the first half of the campaign. A commando light battery with [105 mm Pack Howitzers](/wiki/OTO_Melara_Mod_56 "OTO Melara Mod 56") had deployed to Brunei at the beginning of 1963 but returned to Singapore after a few months when the mopping-up of the Brunei Revolt ended. Despite the escalation in Indonesian attacks after the formation of Malaysia, little need was seen for fire support: the limited range of the guns (10 km (6.2 mi)), the limited availability of helicopters and the size of the country meant that having artillery in the right place at the right time was a challenge. However, a battery from one of the two regiments stationed in Malaysia returned to Borneo in early to mid-1964. These batteries rotated until the end of the confrontation. In early 1965, a complete UK-based regiment arrived. The short-range and substantial weight of the 3-inch mortars meant they were of minimal use.

1966 [ABC](/wiki/Australian_Broadcasting_Corporation "Australian Broadcasting Corporation") report discussing the Indonesian political context of *Konfrontasi*.

Artillery had to adopt new tactics. Almost all guns were deployed in single gun sections within a company or platoon base. The sections were commanded by one of the battery's junior officers, warrant officers or sergeants. Sections had about ten men and did their own technical fire control. They were moved underslung by [Wessex](/wiki/Westland_Wessex "Westland Wessex") or [Belvedere](/wiki/Bristol_Belvedere "Bristol Belvedere") helicopters as necessary to deal with incursions or support operations. Forward observers were in short supply, but it seems that they always accompanied normal infantry [Claret operations](/wiki/Operation_Claret "Operation Claret") and occasionally special forces ones.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] However, artillery observers rarely accompanied patrols inside Sabah and Sarawak unless they were in pursuit of a known incursion and guns were in range. Observation parties were almost always led by an officer and were only two or three men strong.

Communications were a problem; radios were not used within platoons, only rearwards. Ranges were invariably beyond the capability of manpack VHF radios ([A41 and A42](/wiki/Larkspur_radio_system "Larkspur radio system"), copies of AN/PRC 9 and 10), although the use of relay or rebroadcast stations helped where they were tactically possible. Patrol bases could use the World War II vintage [HF No 62 Set](/wiki/Wireless_Set_No._62 "Wireless Set No. 62") (distinguished by having its control panel labelled in English and Russian). Until the manpack A13 arrived in 1966, the only lightweight HF set was the Australian A510, which did not provide voice, only Morse code.

#### British psychological operations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indonesia%E2%80%93Malaysia_confrontation&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: British psychological operations")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/63/Anti_Indonesian_Infiltration_during_Confrontation%2C_1965.jpg/250px-Anti_Indonesian_Infiltration_during_Confrontation%2C_1965.jpg)](/wiki/File:Anti_Indonesian_Infiltration_during_Confrontation,_1965.jpg)

[Anti-Indonesian infiltration](/wiki/Anti-Indonesian_sentiment "Anti-Indonesian sentiment") into Malaysia demonstration by a group of [Malay women](/wiki/Ethnic_Malays "Ethnic Malays") in 1965. The banner reads "Our womenfolk are ready to defend Malaysia. Long live Tengku! Destroy [Sukarno](/wiki/Sukarno "Sukarno")!"

The revelations included an anonymous Foreign Office source stating that the decision to unseat President Sukarno was made by [Prime Minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_the_United_Kingdom "Prime Minister of the United Kingdom") [Harold Macmillan](/wiki/Harold_Macmillan "Harold Macmillan") and then executed under Prime Minister [Harold Wilson](/wiki/Harold_Wilson "Harold Wilson"). According to the exposés, the UK had already become alarmed with the announcement of the "Konfrontasi" policy.[[81]](#cite_note-Lashmar1998-83) It has been claimed that a [Central Intelligence Agency](/wiki/Central_Intelligence_Agency "Central Intelligence Agency") memorandum of 1962 indicated that Macmillan and [US President](/wiki/President_of_the_United_States "President of the United States") [John F. Kennedy](/wiki/John_F._Kennedy "John F. Kennedy") were increasingly alarmed by the possibility of the confrontation with Malaysia spreading, and agreed to "liquidate President Sukarno, depending on the situation and available opportunities".[[82]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBlum2003195-84)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4f/Ganyang_Malaysia_demonstration_PKI_02.jpg/250px-Ganyang_Malaysia_demonstration_PKI_02.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ganyang_Malaysia_demonstration_PKI_02.jpg)

[Anti-Malay](/wiki/Anti-Malay_sentiment "Anti-Malay sentiment") and [Anti-British involvement](/wiki/Anti-British_sentiment "Anti-British sentiment") in North Borneo demonstration by a group of *Pemuda* in 1965, organized by the [Communist Party of Indonesia](/wiki/Communist_Party_of_Indonesia "Communist Party of Indonesia"). The banner reads "Seize British capital! Hang that [Tengku Abdul Rahman](/wiki/Tunku_Abdul_Rahman "Tunku Abdul Rahman")!"

The role of the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom")'s [Foreign Office](/wiki/Foreign_Office "Foreign Office") and MI6 during the confrontation was brought to light in a series of exposés by Paul Lashmar and Oliver James in *[The Independent](/wiki/The_Independent "The Independent")* beginning in 1997 and has also been covered in journals on military and intelligence history.
To weaken the regime, the UK Foreign Office's [Information Research Department](/wiki/Information_Research_Department "Information Research Department") (IRD) coordinated [psychological operations](/wiki/Psychological_operations "Psychological operations") (psyops) in concert with the British military, to spread [black propaganda](/wiki/Black_propaganda "Black propaganda") casting the PKI, [Chinese Indonesians](/wiki/Chinese_Indonesians "Chinese Indonesians"), and Sukarno in a bad light. These efforts were to duplicate the successes of the British psyop campaign in the Malayan Emergency.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

These efforts were coordinated from the British High Commission in Singapore, where the [BBC](/wiki/BBC "BBC"), [Associated Press](/wiki/Associated_Press "Associated Press"), and *[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")* filed their reports on the crisis in Indonesia. According to Roland Challis, the BBC correspondent who was in Singapore at the time, journalists were open to manipulation by the IRD due to Sukarno's refusal to allow them into the country: "In a curious way, by keeping correspondents out of the country Sukarno made them the victims of official channels, because almost the only information you could get was from the British ambassador in Jakarta."[[81]](#cite_note-Lashmar1998-83) These manipulations included the BBC reporting that communists were planning to slaughter the citizens of Jakarta. The accusation was based on a forgery planted by [Norman Reddaway](/wiki/Norman_Reddaway "Norman Reddaway"), a propaganda expert with the IRD. He later bragged in a letter to the British ambassador in Jakarta, Sir [Andrew Gilchrist](/wiki/Andrew_Gilchrist "Andrew Gilchrist"), that it "went all over the world and back again", and was "put almost instantly back into Indonesia via the BBC".[[83]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEaster2004168-85) Gilchrist himself informed the Foreign Office on 5 October 1965: "I have never concealed from you my belief that a little shooting in Indonesia would be an essential preliminary to effective change."[[84]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPilger200333–34-86)

In April 2000, [Denis Healey](/wiki/Denis_Healey "Denis Healey"), [Secretary of State for Defence](/wiki/Secretary_of_State_for_Defence "Secretary of State for Defence") at the time of the war, confirmed that the IRD was active during this time. He officially denied any role by MI6, and denied "personal knowledge" of the British arming of the Army's right-wing faction, though he did comment that if there were such a plan, he "would certainly have supported it".[[85]](#cite_note-TheIndependent-16Apr00-87) Although MI6 was strongly implicated in this scheme by the use of the IRD (seen as an MI6 office), any role by MI6 itself is officially denied by the UK government, and papers relating to it have yet to be declassified by the [Cabinet Office](/wiki/Cabinet_Office "Cabinet Office").[[85]](#cite_note-TheIndependent-16Apr00-87)

#### British Army

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indonesia%E2%80%93Malaysia_confrontation&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: British Army")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0a/Indonesian_M1.jpg/250px-Indonesian_M1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Indonesian_M1.jpg)

Indonesian [M1 Garand rifle](/wiki/M1_Garand_rifle "M1 Garand rifle") (possibly a B59, a modified Garand made by Beretta in Indonesia) captured by the British SAS. [Imperial War Museum](/wiki/Imperial_War_Museum "Imperial War Museum"), [London](/wiki/London "London")

One squadron of the British Army's 22 Special Air Service regiment was deployed to Borneo in early 1963 in the aftermath of the Brunei Revolt to gather information in the border area about Indonesian infiltration.[[86]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDickens200339–55-88) There was a British Army presence until the end of the campaign. Faced with a border of 1,563 kilometres (971 mi), they could not be everywhere, and at this time, 22 SAS had only three squadrons. Also present were the Special Boat Service (SBS) of the Royal Marine Commandos. They had two sections based in Singapore.[[87]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEParker2005154–155-89) Tactical HQ of 22 SAS deployed to Kuching in 1964 to take control of all SAS and SBS operations.[[88]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDickens2003153-90)[[89]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPugsley2003263-91) The shortage of SAS and SBS personnel was exacerbated by the need for them in South Arabia, in many ways, a far more demanding task in challenging conditions against a cunning and aggressive opponent.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The solution was to create new units for Borneo. The first to be employed in Borneo were the Pathfinder Platoon of the Guards Independent Parachute Company, which already existed as the pathfinder force of 16th Parachute Brigade. Next, the Gurkha Independent Parachute Company was raised.[[90]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDickens200372-92) Sections of the SBS were also used, but mostly for amphibious tasks.[[91]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEParker2005153–169-93) Finally, the Parachute Regiment battalions formed patrol companies (C in the 2nd and D in the 3rd).[[92]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEvan_der_Bijl2007174_&_224-94) The situation eased in 1965 when the Australian and New Zealand governments agreed that their forces could be used in Borneo, enabling both Australian and New Zealand SAS squadrons to rotate through Borneo.[[93]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPugsley2003256_&_263-95)

SAS activities were mostly covert reconnaissance and surveillance patrols by four-person teams.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] However, some larger scale raiding missions took place, including amphibious ones by the SBS. Once Claret operations were authorised, most missions were inside Kalimantan, although they conducted operations over the border before Claret from about early 1964.[[94]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPocock1973187_&_196-96)

## End of the conflict

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indonesia%E2%80%93Malaysia_confrontation&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: End of the conflict")]

### Political changes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indonesia%E2%80%93Malaysia_confrontation&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Political changes")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0e/Avro_Vulcan_Malaysia.jpg/250px-Avro_Vulcan_Malaysia.jpg)](/wiki/File:Avro_Vulcan_Malaysia.jpg)

[RAF](/wiki/RAF "RAF") [Avro Vulcan](/wiki/Avro_Vulcan "Avro Vulcan") bomber lands at [RAF Butterworth](/wiki/RAF_Butterworth "RAF Butterworth"), [Malaysia](/wiki/Malaysia "Malaysia"), c 1965. The presence of these [strategic bombers](/wiki/Strategic_bomber "Strategic bomber") was a considerable deterrent to the Indonesians.

By the end of 1965, the conflict was at a stand still. The newly elected President of the Philippines, [Ferdinand Marcos](/wiki/Ferdinand_Marcos "Ferdinand Marcos"), was keen to ease tension and, to some extent, differed from his predecessor by not pursuing the North Sabah claim as fervently. In doing so, the new government planned to recognise the Federation of Malaysia. On February 5, it was announced that the Philippines would inform Indonesia a day in advance of its normalization of relations with Kuala Lumpur. Now truly isolated, Sukarno's denounced Marcos's actions in his speech, much to the surprise of Manila. Sukarno's speech triggered a renewed wave of pledges of support for confrontation, with youth and religious organizations denouncing Marcos's plan.[[95]](#cite_note-Weinstein-2009-97)

> "If Marcos wants to aid Malaysia, that's his business, but we will continue to crush Malaysia, even if we have to fight alone."

— Sukarno, on his opinion of Ferdinand Marcos, [[95]](#cite_note-Weinstein-2009-97)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/57/Volunteers_of_Dwikora.jpg/250px-Volunteers_of_Dwikora.jpg)](/wiki/File:Volunteers_of_Dwikora.jpg)

Indonesian volunteers ready to infiltrate and depart for the [Kalimantan](/wiki/Kalimantan "Kalimantan")-Brunei border in [Balikpapan](/wiki/Balikpapan "Balikpapan") harbour, July 1965.

The confrontation began to ease in 1965 following internal political struggle in Indonesia.
