On 1 October 1965, several senior army generals were kidnapped and killed in an [attempted coup](/wiki/30_September_Movement "30 September Movement") purportedly carried out by the [Indonesian Communist Party](/wiki/Indonesian_Communist_Party "Indonesian Communist Party") (PKI), which was swiftly suppressed by the army under General Suharto.
General Nasution narrowly escaped from his would-be captors. In the ensuing confusion, Sukarno agreed to allow Suharto to assume emergency command and control of Jakarta and the armed forces stationed there. Blame for the failed coup was attributed to the [Indonesian Communist Party](/wiki/Indonesian_Communist_Party "Indonesian Communist Party") (PKI), and in the following weeks and months a campaign of [imprisonment and lynching](/wiki/Indonesian_mass_killings_of_1965%E2%80%9366 "Indonesian mass killings of 1965–66") of PKI members and sympathisers broke out across Jakarta and Indonesia.[[96]](#cite_note-nlb-98) With Suharto's grip on power in Jakarta and Indonesia delicately poised, the scale and intensity of Indonesia's campaign of infiltrations into East Borneo began to ease.[[97]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPocock1973215-99)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/aa/Australian_soldier_Borneo.jpg/250px-Australian_soldier_Borneo.jpg)](/wiki/File:Australian_soldier_Borneo.jpg)

An Australian soldier manning an [L7 General Purpose Machine Gun](/wiki/FN_MAG "FN MAG") (GPMG) at a forward position.

Claret operations nevertheless, continued and, in March, a Gurkha battalion was involved in some of the fiercest fighting of the campaign during two raids into Kalimantan.[[98]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPocock1973213–214-100) Minor action by Indonesian forces continued in the border area, including an attempt at counter-battery fire against a 105 mm gun position in Central Brigade (reports from locals said the British return fire had turned over the Indonesian gun, thought to be 76 mm).

Following this [leadership change](/wiki/Transition_to_the_New_Order "Transition to the New Order"), Indonesian interest in continuing the confrontation declined and military activity diminished.[[99]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGoldsworthy2001342-101) Earlier efforts by Japan, Thailand, and the Philippines to mediate between 1964 and 1965 had failed. In April 1966, the new Indonesian leadership began signalling its openness to peace talks, which formally commenced.

### Final actions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indonesia%E2%80%93Malaysia_confrontation&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Final actions")]

At the same time with Indonesia's political hiatus beginning to stabilise (it had stopped a major RPKAD operation to capture a British prisoner), the RPKAD linked up with PGRS to establish guerrilla forces in Sabah and Sarawak. The Sabah effort never crossed the border; however, two groups entered Sarawak in February and May and obtained support from local sympathisers. The first group, despite losses in several contacts, lasted until June and exfiltrated on hearing about the end of Konfrontasi. Survivors of the second, after contact with Australian troops, also made it back to Indonesia.[[100]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEConboy2003158–161-102)

The final Indonesian incursion was in May and June. Signs of a substantial force were found crossing into Central Brigade. This was some 80 strong, mostly volunteers, led by Lieutenant Sombi (or Sumbi) and a team from 600 Raider Company. They moved fast towards Brunei with [1/7 Gurkhas](/wiki/7th_Duke_of_Edinburgh%27s_Own_Gurkha_Rifles "7th Duke of Edinburgh's Own Gurkha Rifles") pursuing and ambushing them; almost all were accounted for. In response to this, a final Claret operation was launched, which was a successful artillery ambush by 38 Light Battery.

### Treaty

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indonesia%E2%80%93Malaysia_confrontation&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Treaty")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/77/Signing_of_the_Jakarta_Accords_11_August_1966.jpg/250px-Signing_of_the_Jakarta_Accords_11_August_1966.jpg)](/wiki/File:Signing_of_the_Jakarta_Accords_11_August_1966.jpg)

[Tun Abdul Razak](/wiki/Abdul_Razak_Hussein "Abdul Razak Hussein") and [Adam Malik](/wiki/Adam_Malik "Adam Malik") signing the Bangkok Agreement, 11 August 1966.[[101]](#cite_note-HistoryGov-103) The end of confrontation marks the [beginning](/wiki/ASEAN_Declaration "ASEAN Declaration") of [ASEAN](/wiki/ASEAN "ASEAN").

On 28 May, at a conference in [Bangkok](/wiki/Bangkok "Bangkok"), the Malaysian and Indonesian governments declared the conflict was over. However, it was unclear if [Suharto](/wiki/Suharto "Suharto") was in full control of Indonesia (rather than Sukarno), and vigilance in Borneo could not be relaxed. By June, both sides had agreed in principle to a peace settlement, following meetings between [Malaysian Deputy Prime Minister](/wiki/Deputy_Prime_Minister_of_Malaysia "Deputy Prime Minister of Malaysia") [Tun Abdul Razak](/wiki/Abdul_Razak_Hussein "Abdul Razak Hussein") and [Indonesian Minister of Foreign Affairs](/wiki/Ministry_of_Foreign_Affairs_(Indonesia)#List_of_foreign_ministers "Ministry of Foreign Affairs (Indonesia)") [Adam Malik](/wiki/Adam_Malik "Adam Malik").[[96]](#cite_note-nlb-98)

With Suharto's co-operation a peace treaty was signed on 11 August and ratified two days later,[[8]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarver1986806-10) ending the undeclared war.[[101]](#cite_note-HistoryGov-103)[[102]](#cite_note-104)[[103]](#cite_note-Historia-105) The treaty provided for the cessation of hostilities, the immediate restoration of diplomatic relations, and an opportunity for the people of [Sabah](/wiki/Sabah "Sabah") and [Sarawak](/wiki/Sarawak "Sarawak") to reaffirm their position within Malaysia through democratic processes.

## Aftermath

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indonesia%E2%80%93Malaysia_confrontation&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Aftermath")]

Although the Indonesians had conducted a few amphibious raids and an airborne operation against Malaya, the war remained limited throughout its duration and remained largely a land conflict. For either side to have escalated to large scale air or naval attacks "would have incurred disadvantages greatly outweighing the marginal military effect that they might have produced".[[8]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarver1986806-10) For Indonesia and Sukarno, the military and diplomatic elements of the confrontation came to nothing - the Commonwealth forces easily defeated every single of the latters incursions. For Malaysia the Indonesian hostility cemented their national identity rather than undermining it. After Indonesia had left the United Nations, none of their allies followed their leaving a period of diminished influence in the Non-Aligned Movement.[[104]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEClarkPietsch201422-106)

For Britain the conflict had been an unqualified success.[[105]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEvan_der_Bijl2007246-107) The UK Secretary of State for Defence at the time, Denis Healey, described the campaign as "one of the most efficient uses of military forces in the history of the world".[[106]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPimlott198499-108) A rising Communist power in Asia had been subdued and prevented the 'domino effect' from taking off. From a purely tactical standpoint, it had been a strong performance from British troops and commanders, as they had managed to confine a small bush war with relatively small human cost and stabilised a potentially dangerous political situation in Southeast Asia.[[107]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAllen2016159-109)

For the benefit of the West, an anti-communist purge spread throughout Indonesia. Suharto's steady consolidation of power after the events of 30 September allowed him to form a [new government](/wiki/Transition_to_the_New_Order "Transition to the New Order") and in March 1967 Suharto was able to form a new cabinet that excluded Sukarno.

For Indonesia and Malaysia, the end of Konfrontasi contributed to the normalization of regional relations after several years of confrontation, forming a basis of order and enabling them to coexist peacefully. This coexistence was shaped by common identities and shared strategic interests, which together fostered closer relations between Indonesia and Malaysia. In this context, both states worked to create a more friendly and cooperative regional climate in Southeast Asia, helping to lay the groundwork for subsequent regional cooperation, including the [establishment](/wiki/History_of_ASEAN "History of ASEAN") of [ASEAN](/wiki/ASEAN "ASEAN") in 1967.[[96]](#cite_note-nlb-98)[[108]](#cite_note-110)

British operations such as Claret were only publicly disclosed by Britain in 1974, while the Australian government did not officially acknowledge its involvement until 1996.[[109]](#cite_note-111)[[110]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECoates2006333-112)

British Commonwealth forces peaked at 17,000 deployed in Borneo, with another 10,000 more available in Malaya and Singapore.[[8]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarver1986806-10)

The withdrawal parade in [Labuan](/wiki/Labuan "Labuan") from the [Royal Navy](/wiki/Royal_Navy "Royal Navy"), [Royal Australian Navy](/wiki/Royal_Australian_Navy "Royal Australian Navy") and [Royal New Zealand Navy](/wiki/Royal_New_Zealand_Navy "Royal New Zealand Navy") at the end of the confrontation after their successful mission.

Total British Commonwealth military casualties were 280 killed and 180 wounded, the greatest number of them were British.[[8]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarver1986806-10) Gurkha losses were 43 killed and 83 wounded, losses among other British armed forces were 19 killed and 44 wounded. Australian casualties were 16 killed, of whom 7 were killed in action, and 9 wounded. New Zealand casualties were 7 killed and another 7 wounded or injured.[[111]](#cite_note-113)[[112]](#cite_note-AWM_war_casualties-114) The remaining casualties were that of the Malaysian military, police, and Border Scouts. A significant number of British casualties occurred during helicopter accidents, including a Belvedere crash that killed several SAS commanders and a Foreign Office official, possibly a member of MI6. A Wessex collision also killed several men from 2nd Parachute Battalion, and a Westland Scout crash, on 16 July 1964, near Kluang airfield, killed the two crewmen from 656 Sqn AAC. Finally, in August 1966, there remained two British and two Australian soldiers missing and presumed dead, with the Australians (both from the SASR) having probably drowned while crossing a swollen river.[[113]](#cite_note-AWM_2nd_Squad_SAS-115) The remains of a Royal Marine were recovered some 20 years later.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Altogether, 36 civilians were killed, 53 wounded and 4 captured, with most being local inhabitants.[[8]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarver1986806-10)

Indonesian casualties were estimated at 590 killed, 222 wounded and 771 captured.[[8]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarver1986806-10)

### Awards

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Indonesia%E2%80%93Malaysia_confrontation&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Awards")]

A number of British Commonwealth soldiers were awarded gallantry awards for actions during the campaign. No Distinguished Flying Cross or naval awards were made.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

All Indonesian military or police personnel involved in the conflict were given the [Northern Borneo Military Campaign Medal](/wiki/Orders,_decorations,_and_medals_of_Indonesia#Obsolete_decorations_for_military_service "Orders, decorations, and medals of Indonesia") (*Satyalancana Wira Dharma*),[[114]](#cite_note-116)[[115]](#cite_note-117) and two Indonesian marines ([Usman](/wiki/Usman_Haji_Muhammad_Ali "Usman Haji Muhammad Ali") and [Harun](/wiki/Harun_Thohir "Harun Thohir")) were honoured as [National Heroes of Indonesia](/wiki/National_Hero_of_Indonesia "National Hero of Indonesia").
